title: LLMOps & GenAI Serving — vLLM, Triton Inference Server & RAG at Scale
subtitle: How FAANG-scale AI serving works under the hood — and how SAP's generative AI (Joule, AI Core) maps to production inference infrastructure

sections.content:
## The GenAI Serving Problem

Training large language models gets all the attention, but **inference serving** is where the real engineering challenges lie:

- **Latency:** Users expect LLM responses in < 2 seconds, not 30 seconds
- **Throughput:** A popular feature can receive 10,000+ requests per second
- **Cost:** GPU inference is 10-100x more expensive than CPU serving
- **Memory:** A 70B parameter model requires 140GB+ of GPU memory (FP16)
- **Scalability:** Serving must handle traffic spikes without collapsing

For Staff+ engineers: GenAI serving infrastructure is the most impactful problem to work on in 2024-2026. Every FAANG company is hiring for it. Understanding the stack — vLLM, Triton Inference Server, RAG pipelines, GPU optimization — is a career-defining skill.

For SAP architects: SAP's Joule copilot and AI Core use similar infrastructure under the hood. Understanding how the inference layer works helps you design better SAP integrations with LLMs.

cards.title: vLLM — High-Throughput LLM Serving
cards.body:
vLLM (UC Berkeley) is the most popular open-source LLM serving engine. Key innovations:

**PagedAttention** — The KV cache for each request is fragmented across non-contiguous memory blocks (like OS virtual memory pages). This eliminates the 60-80% memory waste from fragmentation, enabling 2-4x higher throughput.

**Continuous batching** — Unlike traditional batch processing (wait for N requests, then process), vLLM adds new requests to the running batch after each iteration completes. This maximizes GPU utilization.

**Prefix caching** — If multiple requests share a common prompt prefix (e.g., system prompt), the KV cache for that prefix is shared, saving computation.

**Result:** vLLM serves 2-4x more tokens/second than HuggingFace TGI and 2x more than NVIDIA Triton for pure LLM workloads.

cards.title: NVIDIA Triton Inference Server
cards.body:
Triton is NVIDIA's production inference platform. Unlike vLLM (LLM-only), Triton serves **any model type**: LLMs, vision models, embedding models, and ensemble pipelines.

**Model concurrency** — Multiple models on the same GPU, with dynamic batching per model. An LLM and an embedding model can share the same GPU.

**Model ensembles** — Chain multiple models into a single inference pipeline (e.g., text -> embedding -> classifier). Triton manages the data flow between models.

**Performance tuning:**
- Dynamic batching (max batch size, delay timer)
- Concurrent model execution (GPU streams)
- Model warmup (pre-load KV cache)
- GPU memory pooling (CUDA unified memory)

**Tradeoff:** Triton is more complex to configure but more flexible. vLLM is simpler and faster for pure LLM serving.

cards.title: RAG — Retrieval-Augmented Generation at Scale
cards.body:
RAG is the dominant pattern for production LLM applications. The pipeline:

1. **Ingestion** — Chunk documents, generate embeddings (Nomic, OpenAI, Cohere), store in vector DB
2. **Retrieval** — Convert user query to embedding, search vector DB for top-K similar chunks
3. **Augmentation** — Combine retrieved chunks + query into a prompt
4. **Generation** — LLM generates response based on augmented prompt

**Scaling challenges:**
- Embedding generation: Use a separate Triton endpoint or batching server
- Vector DB latency: HNSW indexing for sub-10ms search at 10M+ vectors
- Context window limits: Chunking strategy matters (200-500 token chunks, 50% overlap)
- Caching: Embedding cache (same query -> same embedding) and LLM response cache (identical queries)

**FAANG approach:** Dedicated embedding service (Triton + E5/Llama embeddings) + distributed vector search (ScaNN, DiskANN) + vLLM for generation.

cards.title: RAG Evaluation & Quality
cards.body:
RAG quality depends on retrieval accuracy + generation quality. Key metrics:

**Retrieval Metrics:**
- Hit Rate: Percentage of queries where relevant context is retrieved
- MRR (Mean Reciprocal Rank): Rank of first relevant result
- NDCG: Rank-weighted relevance (discounts lower ranks)

**Generation Metrics:**
- Faithfulness: Does the response stick to the retrieved context?
- Answer Relevance: Does the response address the query?
- Context Precision: How much of the context was actually used?

**Tools:** RAGAS framework, TruLens, LangSmith.

**FAANG practice:** Continuous evaluation pipeline — sample 1% of production queries, have LLM-as-judge score each response, alert on score degradation.

sections.title: LLM Inference Optimization Techniques
rows: Default. Use BF16 for newer GPUs (A100, H100)
Memory linear in sequence length (not quadratic)
For models that don't fit on one GPU (70B+)
When many requests share a system prompt

sections.title: RAG Serving Architecture (FAANG-Style)
sections.code:
-- Production RAG pipeline architecture --

Client Request
  |
  v
[Gateway / Load Balancer]
  |  -> Rate limiting (per-user, per-API-key)
  |  -> Authentication + Authorization
  v
[RAG Orchestrator Service]
  |  - Classifies query type (factual vs creative vs code)
  |  - Routes to appropriate pipeline
  |
  |--- 1. Query Rewriting ---
  |    LLM rewrites ambiguous query: 'latest features' -> 'new features in v3.2.0'
  |
  |--- 2. Embedding ---
  |    Triton server: bge-large-en-v1.5 (FP16, batch size 32)
  |    Time: ~50ms per query
  |
  |--- 3. Vector Search ---
  |    Vector DB: Pinecone / Qdrant / Milvus (HNSW index)
  |    Top-K: 5 chunks (200 tokens each, 50% overlap)
  |    Time: ~10ms (10M vectors, 768 dims, ef_search=100)
  |
  |--- 4. Context Assembly ---
  |    Combine chunks + query + system prompt
  |    Truncate if total > 4K tokens (context limit)
  |
  |--- 5. Generation ---
  |    vLLM server: Llama 3 70B (FP16, tensor parallel 4)
  |    Temperature: 0.1 (factual), 0.7 (creative)
  |    Max tokens: 1024
  |    Time: ~500ms (first token) + ~50ms/token
  |
  |--- 6. Post-processing ---
  |    Citation extraction: [1], [2] -> hyperlinks
  |    Answer relevance filter: reject if answer doesn't address query
  |
  v
Client Response (~1-2 seconds total)

sections.content:
## SAP Bridge — Joule, AI Core & GenAI in the SAP Ecosystem

### SAP's GenAI Infrastructure

SAP has built its GenAI infrastructure around three layers:

**SAP AI Core** — The ML platform (model hosting, training, serving). Supports HuggingFace models, SageMaker integration, and custom containers. Behind the scenes, it uses Kubernetes + GPU nodes (not unlike FAANG's internal platforms).

**SAP AI Launchpad** — The UI for managing AI scenarios, deployments, and monitoring.

**SAP Joule** — The copilot interface. Joule calls AI Core models, embeds into SAP applications (S/4HANA, SuccessFactors, Ariba).

### The Gap vs FAANG

| Aspect | SAP AI Core | FAANG Inference Platform |
|---|---|---|
| Serving engine | HuggingFace TGI (default), custom containers | vLLM (LLMs), Triton (multi-model) |
| GPU optimization | Minimal (generic containers) | PagedAttention, Flash Attention, quantization |
| Batching | Request-level (one request at a time) | Continuous batching (max GPU utilization) |
| RAG support | AI Core's vector engine (HANA Vector) | Dedicated embedding service + vector DB |
| Monitoring | Basic metrics | Per-request latency breakdown, GPU utilization, quality scores |
| Cost optimization | N/A (fixed infrastructure) | Spot GPU, model distillation, speculative decoding |

### What SAP Architects Should Know

1. **Joule uses RAG internally** — Joule retrieves context from SAP Help Portal, SAP Community, and customer-specific knowledge bases before generating responses. Understanding RAG quality directly impacts Joule adoption.
2. **HANA Vector Engine** — SAP's vector DB inside HANA Cloud enables RAG without external infrastructure. Know the tradeoffs (HANA's hnsw implementation vs Pinecone/Qdrant).
3. **Model selection matters** — Joule uses multiple models (GPT-4 for complex queries, smaller models for simple classification). This 'model routing' pattern is exactly what FAANG serving platforms implement.
4. **The SAP use case is uniquely suited** — Enterprise data is mostly structured + semi-structured (SAP tables, IDocs). RAG for enterprise requires table-aware chunking (entity extraction, relationship preservation) that general-purpose RAG doesn't handle well.

tabs.label: Architecture
tabs.content:
**Interviewer:** "Design an LLM serving platform for a customer support chatbot."

**Staff+ answer:** "I'd use a three-tier architecture:

**Tier 1: Gateway** — Authentication, rate limiting (per-user: 10 req/min, per-API-key: 1000 req/min), load balancing across model replicas, request queue for overload protection.

**Tier 2: RAG Pipeline** — Query classification (intent + complexity), query rewriting, embedding generation (Triton with E5-large), vector search (Pinecone, 10M+ docs, HNSW index, sub-10ms), context assembly with token budget management.

**Tier 3: Generation** — Model replicas behind vLLM, each with PagedAttention + continuous batching. Two model tiers: small model (Llama 3 8B, FP16, single GPU) for 80% of queries, large model (Llama 3 70B, FP16, TP=4) for complex queries. Model routing based on query complexity score.

**Observability:** Per-request latency breakdown (embedding time, search time, generation time), GPU utilization per model replica, cache hit rate (KV cache + response cache), and quality scores from LLM-as-judge evaluation on 1% sample.

**Cost optimization:** Spot GPU for non-latency-sensitive batch workloads, reserved GPU for real-time generation, cache TTL tuning."

**Why this impresses:** You gave a complete architecture with three tiers, specific technologies (Triton, vLLM, Pinecone), two model tiers with routing, and cost optimization strategy. This is a production-ready design.

tabs.label: Performance
tabs.content:
**Interviewer:** "Your LLM serving is too slow (P95 = 8 seconds). How do you debug and fix it?"

**Staff+ answer:** "First, break down latency into components:

1. **Network + auth:** Expect < 50ms. If higher, check load balancer and auth service.
2. **RAG pipeline:** Embedding (~50ms) + vector search (~10ms) + context assembly (~5ms). Should be < 100ms total. If higher, check embedding batch size or vector DB index quality.
3. **Time-to-first-token (TTFT):** Should be < 500ms. If higher, check prompt size (long prompts take longer to prefill), model parallelism overhead, or GPU memory bandwidth contention.
4. **Inter-token latency:** Should be < 50ms/token. If higher, check GPU utilization (are other models sharing the GPU?), KV cache fragmentation, or model quantization level.

**Most common fixes:**
- Enable PagedAttention (vLLM) — reduces TTFT by 50%
- Reduce max sequence length — shorter sequences = less KV cache pressure
- Increase batch size — continuous batching works best with more concurrent requests
- Switch to INT4 quantization — 2x tokens/second with acceptable quality loss
- Use speculative decoding — draft model generates tokens, target model verifies = 2x tokens/second

**Last resort:** Add more GPU replicas. But always optimize the model before scaling horizontally."

**Why this impresses:** You gave a systematic debugging approach (break down P95 into components with expected values), specific fixes with expected impact, and the principle (optimize before scaling).

tabs.label: SAP Integration
tabs.content:
**Interviewer:** "How would you integrate an LLM with SAP S/4HANA for natural-language querying?"

**Staff+ answer:** "This is Text-to-SQL + RAG combined. Architecture:

1. **Schema ingestion** — Crawl SAP DDIC (Data Dictionary) tables, extract table names, field names, data types, relationships, and descriptions. Store as embeddings in HANA Vector Engine.
2. **Query understanding** — User asks 'Show me top 10 customers by revenue this quarter.' Classify intent, extract entities (customers, revenue, quarter).
3. **Schema retrieval** — Retrieve relevant tables/fields from HANA Vector Engine (KNA1 for customers, VBAK for sales orders, VBAP for line items, etc.).
4. **Context + prompt** — Build prompt with: retrieved schema definitions (field names + descriptions), user query, examples of correct Text-to-SQL for similar patterns.
5. **SQL generation** — LLM generates ABAP SQL or HANA SQL. Use a model fine-tuned on SAP SQL patterns (or append SAP SQL examples to the prompt).
6. **SQL validation** — Parse the generated SQL, validate table/field names against DDIC, reject and retry if invalid.
7. **Execute + format** — Run SQL against HANA, format results as a natural language response with citations.

**Key challenge:** SAP's data model is complex (thousands of tables, cryptic field names like VBAP-NETWR). The embedding for 'customer name' might match multiple fields (KNA1-NAME1, ADR6-MC_NAME1, etc.). The LLM needs to handle ambiguity gracefully."

**Why this impresses:** You gave a concrete 7-step architecture with SAP-specific challenges (DDIC, cryptic field names, ambiguous entity resolution). Shows you understand the real difficulty of enterprise LLM integration.

tabs.label: Evaluation
tabs.content:
**Interviewer:** "How do you evaluate whether your GenAI feature is working well in production?"

**Staff+ answer:** "Three tiers of metrics:

**Tier 1: System metrics** (always-on)
- P50/P95/P99 latency (target: < 2s P95)
- Tokens per second per GPU (target: > 50 tokens/s for 7B, > 20 tokens/s for 70B)
- Request error rate (target: < 0.1% 5xx errors)
- Queue depth (target: < 100 requests waiting at peak)

**Tier 2: Quality metrics** (continuous sampling)
- LLM-as-judge scores on 1% of production requests
- Faithfulness (does answer stick to context?)
- Helpfulness (does answer address the query?)
- Safety (no harmful/toxic content)

**Tier 3: Business metrics** (weekly dashboard)
- User retention: Do users return after their first query?
- Task completion rate: Did the user achieve their goal?
- Human escalation rate: How often does the user need a human agent?
- Cost per query: Infrastructure cost divided by query count

**The most important metric** for a Staff+ engineer: cost per successful query. If each query costs $0.05 and has a 90% success rate, the effective cost per successful query is $0.056. Reducing model size, improving RAG accuracy, and caching can all improve this metric."

**Why this impresses:** You gave a three-tier evaluation framework with specific targets and the Staff+ meta-metric (cost per successful query). Shows you think about GenAI as a business, not just a technology.

cards.title: vLLM + PagedAttention Are the Standard
cards.body: vLLM's PagedAttention eliminates KV cache fragmentation, delivering 2-4x throughput improvement over naive serving. It's the de facto standard for open-source LLM serving.

cards.title: Triton for Multi-Model Serving
cards.body: When you need to serve LLMs + embedding models + vision models on the same GPU, Triton is the choice. It's more complex but more flexible than vLLM.

cards.title: RAG Is the Dominant Production Pattern
cards.body: Most production LLM features use RAG, not raw LLM calls. The retrieval pipeline (embedding + vector search) is often the bottleneck, not the LLM itself.

cards.title: Quantization Is Your Cost Saver
cards.body: INT8/INT4 quantization reduces GPU memory by 75-87% with minimal quality loss. Use it for high-throughput, low-latency deployments where every millisecond counts.

cards.title: Continuous Batching Maximizes GPU
cards.body: Traditional request-level batching underutilizes GPU memory. Continuous batching (add requests to the active batch after each iteration) is essential for production throughput.

cards.title: Cost Per Successful Query Is the Key Metric
cards.body: Model serving cost + RAG cost + error rate = effective cost per good response. Optimizing all three together, not just the model, drives the best business outcome.
